hero.title: Solomon's Temple
hero.subtitle: "From the Gold of the First Temple to the Light of the Third"
hero.description: Journey into the heart of ancient Jerusalem — into the sacred chambers where heaven and earth were said to meet. This documentary explores Solomon's legendary Temple: its breathtaking architecture, its sacred objects, its catastrophic destruction, and the timeless Jewish hope for its restoration.
hero.credit: The historical accuracy was guided by the expert consultation of Dr. David Jeselsohn, whose expertise contributed significantly to the reliability of the presentation.

intro.title: A Structure Unlike Any Other
intro.text: Around 950 BCE, King Solomon erected the Beit HaMikdash – Solomon’s Temple – on Mount Moriah — the same rock where Abraham was tested and, according to tradition, where creation itself began. For nearly four centuries, this Temple served as the spiritual center of the Jewish people, and its memory has endured for three millennia.

card1.title: The Temple Courtyard (the Azara)
card1.text: This inner court is called Azara in rabbinic literature. It formed the center of the entire Temple complex, the area where the Kohanim – priests performed the daily service and brought the offerings. The name Azara is derived in the Talmud (Erchin 11a) from the Hebrew word Ezra – help, because whoever entered this court found spiritual help and purification.

card2.title: The Sanctuary (the Heichal)
card2.text: In the Heichal stood the holy vessels of daily service: the golden lampstands – Menorot, the tables of showbread – Shulchanot Lechem haPanim –, and the incense altar of pure gold – Mizbach haKetoret. Here rose the fragrance of the Ketoret – the holy incense – as commanded in Shemot Chapter 30, Verses 7–8.

card3.title: The Holy of Holies (Kodesh haKodashim)
card3.text: The Holy of Holies – Kodesh haKodashim: A cube of gold: 20 × 20 × 20 cubits – perfectly symmetrical. Two golden Cherubim, five meters tall, their wings spanning the entire width. No window, no earthly light – only the Or haShechina. Here rested the Ark of the Covenant. Here heaven and earth touched. The place from which the world was founded.

quoteBanner.quote: From the Ark, say the sages, emanated the spiritual life of the world.

download.label: Resources
download.title: Download the Full Document
download.text: Get the complete documentary companion guide with detailed descriptions, historical timelines, and scriptural references.

blog.label: Documentary · History · Sacred Architecture
blog.title: Solomon's Temple
blog.subtitle: 20 scenes through the holiest structure of antiquity — from the courtyards to the Holy of Holies

s1.title: Jerusalem Today / Mount Moriah – The Place of the Sanctuary
s1.subtitle: Even haSchetijah
s1.source: Bereschit 22 · Divrej haJamim Bet 3:1 · Talmud Yoma 54b
body: Mount Moriah is the place where Abraham sought to offer his son Yitzchak as a sacrifice — the supreme test of faith recorded in Bereschit 22. According to the Talmud (Yoma 54b), this very rock is the Even haSchetijah, the Foundation Stone, the point from which the creation of the world began. It is the axis mundi of Jewish tradition, the place where heaven and earth converge.

Today the Dome of the Rock rises above this ancient summit, its golden cupola visible across the city. But beneath the surface, the memory endures: here stood the Beit HaMikdash, the House of Holiness, the dwelling place chosen by the Divine Presence on earth. For the Jewish people, Mount Moriah has never ceased to be the holiest place on earth.

s2.title: The Temple of Solomon
s2.subtitle: Beit HaMikdash
body: In the fourth year of his reign, King Solomon began the construction of the Temple — a project that would take seven years to complete. The finest cedarwood was brought from Lebanon, cut and prepared by the craftsmen of King Chiram of Tyre. The stones were quarried and shaped at a distance, so that no sound of hammer, chisel, or any iron tool was heard on the Temple Mount during construction.

This silence was not merely practical; it was sacred. Iron, the metal of war, was forbidden in the house of peace. Every block arrived perfectly fitted, every beam precisely measured. The entire structure rose as if placed by unseen hands — a dwelling not built by force, but by devotion.

s3.title: The First Gate to the Outer Court
s3.subtitle: Schaar haChitzon
body: The outer gate marked the threshold between the profane world and the sacred precinct. To pass through it was to leave behind the noise and commerce of Jerusalem and enter a realm set apart for the service of the Almighty. The gate was massive, framed in stone and overlaid with bronze, signaling to all who approached that they stood at the boundary of holiness.

From here, the pilgrim would begin the ascent through successive courts, each one more restricted, each one drawing the worshipper closer to the presence of G-d. The outer court was open to all of Israel — men, women, and children — a place of gathering and prayer.

s4.title: The Wall of the Courtyard
s4.subtitle: Chomat haAzara
body: The courtyard wall rose high above the surrounding terrain, enclosing the sacred precincts in a formidable barrier of hewn stone. According to Melachim Alef 6:36, the inner court was constructed of three rows of dressed stone and one row of cedar beams — a structural technique that also served a symbolic purpose: stone for permanence, cedar for fragrance and sanctity.

Three gates pierced this wall, oriented to the cardinal directions: east, south, and north. The eastern gate — the Nikanor Gate — held particular significance, for through it the first rays of dawn entered the Temple precincts, and through it the Kohen Gadol would one day pass on Yom Kippur to perform the most sacred rites.
measurements: Three gates by cardinal direction: East (Nikanor Gate), South, North

s5.title: The Second Gate to the Priestly Court
s5.subtitle: Azara
body: Beyond the outer court lay the Azara — the priestly court — accessible only to the Kohanim and the Levites who served in the Temple. This was the domain of sacred service, where the daily offerings were prepared and brought before G-d. The Talmud (Erchin 11a) derives the name Azara from the word Ezra, meaning help, for whoever entered this court found spiritual assistance and purification.

The Levites stood at the boundary and sang the Psalms of the daily service, their voices rising above the courts in melodies that tradition says moved heaven itself. Here the smoke of the offerings mingled with the song of the Levites, creating an atmosphere of profound holiness.

s6.title: The Overall Layout of the Temple
s6.subtitle: Beit haMikdash – House of Holiness
body: The Temple proper consisted of three chambers arranged in sequence along an east-west axis: the Ulam (vestibule), the Heichal (sanctuary), and the Kodesh haKodashim (Holy of Holies). This tripartite division mirrored a cosmic progression — from the visible world, through the realm of sacred service, into the innermost dwelling of the Divine Presence.

The structure measured 60 cubits in length, 20 cubits in width, and 30 cubits in height. The vestibule alone soared to 120 cubits — a tower of gold visible from far beyond the city walls. The Midrash teaches that the Temple was conceived as a reflection of the heavenly sanctuary: as above, so below.
s6.quote: "As above, so below – a house for the King in the heights and a house for the King on earth."

s7.title: The Burnt Offering Altar
s7.subtitle: Mizbach haOlah
s7.source: Divrej haJamim Bet 4:1 · Vayikra 6:6 · Talmud Yoma 21a
body: At the heart of the priestly court stood the great altar of burnt offering — the Mizbach haOlah. Constructed of unhewn stone and overlaid with bronze, it dominated the courtyard with its imposing dimensions. Upon this altar, a fire burned perpetually, never extinguished, as commanded in Vayikra 6:6: the fire shall burn continually; it shall not go out.

The Talmud (Yoma 21a) records that five miraculous signs were associated with the altar fire: it lay upon the wood like a lion; the fire was bright as sunlight; it consumed both wet and dry wood alike; it produced no smoke; and the wind could not disturb it. A great ramp, the Kevesh, led up to the altar from the south, for no steps were permitted — the Kohanim ascended in reverence, never exposing themselves before the altar of G-d.

s8.title: The Ten Lavers on Bronze Carts
s8.subtitle: haKiyorim al haMechonot
body: Along the sides of the priestly court stood ten ornate bronze lavers, each mounted on an intricately decorated wheeled stand. These lavers were used for the ritual washing of the offerings before they were brought to the altar. The stands themselves were works of extraordinary craftsmanship, adorned with images of lions, oxen, and cherubim — symbols of majesty, service, and the heavenly realm.

Each stand was built to precise dimensions, with bronze wheels that allowed the heavy basins to be moved as needed. The capacity of each basin — 40 Bat, approximately 450 to 500 liters — testifies to the scale of the daily Temple service and the vast quantities of water required for the purification of offerings.

s9.title: The Great Bronze Sea
s9.subtitle: Jam haNechoshet
body: The Jam haNechoshet — the Great Bronze Sea — was a colossal basin of cast bronze, resting upon twelve bronze oxen arranged in four groups of three, each group facing one of the cardinal directions. It stood in the southeastern corner of the priestly court and served as the primary vessel for the priestly ablutions: the Kohanim washed their hands and feet here before performing the Temple service.

The sheer scale of this vessel was astonishing. With a diameter of ten cubits and a height of five cubits, it held approximately three thousand Bat — some 60,000 liters of water. Its rim was fashioned in the shape of a lily blossom, and its surface was a handbreadth thick. The twelve oxen beneath it symbolized the twelve tribes of Israel, bearing the weight of holiness upon their backs.

s10.title: The Two Pillars Jachin and Boaz
s10.subtitle: Amudej Jachin u Boaz
s10.source: Melachim Alef 7:15–22 · Radak, Ralbag on Melachim Alef 7:21
body: Two mighty bronze pillars flanked the entrance to the Ulam, the vestibule of the Temple. The right pillar was named Jachin — meaning "He will establish" — and the left was named Boaz — meaning "In Him is strength." Together they proclaimed the eternal covenant: G-d will establish His house, and in Him alone resides true power.

Each pillar stood eighteen cubits tall, crowned by an elaborate capital of five cubits featuring rows of pomegranates, lily-work, and lattice chains. Remarkably, these pillars bore no structural load whatsoever. They were purely symbolic — monuments to the divine promise made to the house of David and to the people of Israel.

s11.title: The Gate to the Vestibule
s11.subtitle: Schaar haUlam
s11.source: Melachim Alef 6:33 · Radak on Melachim Alef 6:3
body: The gate to the Ulam marked the final threshold before entering the Temple building itself. Its doorposts were made of olive wood, and the doors were fashioned from cypress, richly carved with images of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers — all overlaid with gold hammered to conform to the carved work beneath.

To pass through this gate was to leave the open sky of the courtyard behind and enter the enclosed world of the sacred building. From this point forward, only Kohanim in active service were permitted. The air changed; the light dimmed; the fragrance of the Ketoret drifted outward. One was now standing at the very threshold of the house of G-d.

s12.title: The Golden Vestibule
s12.subtitle: Ulam
body: The Ulam was the entrance hall of the Temple, a towering chamber of extraordinary height. According to Divrej haJamim Bet 3:4, it rose to 120 cubits — approximately 63 meters — making it one of the tallest structures in the ancient Near East. Its interior walls were overlaid entirely with pure gold, gleaming in the light that entered through the great doorway.

This soaring vestibule served as a transitional space between the outer world and the innermost sanctuaries. It impressed upon every entering Kohen the overwhelming majesty of the place he was about to serve in. The gold that covered its walls was not mere decoration — it was a reflection of the heavenly glory that the Temple was built to house.

s13.title: The Sanctuary
s13.subtitle: Heichal
body: The Heichal — the Sanctuary — was the main hall of the Temple, the chamber where the daily sacred service was performed. Its walls were lined with cedarwood from Lebanon, carved with gourds and open flowers, and overlaid with the finest gold. No stone was visible from within; the entire interior shimmered in golden light.

The windows of the Heichal were unique in all of architecture: narrow on the inside and wide on the outside. Unlike ordinary windows that draw light inward, these were designed to send light outward — for the light of Israel was meant to illuminate the world, not to receive illumination from it. The Sanctuary measured 40 cubits in length, 20 in width, and 30 in height.
s13.quote: The windows were "narrow on the inside, wide on the outside" – the light of Israel was meant to illuminate the world, not itself.

s14.title: The Golden Lampstands
s14.subtitle: Menorot
s14.source: Melachim Alef 7:49 · Talmud Menachot 98b · Rambam Hilchot Beit haBechira 3:7
body: Ten golden lampstands stood within the Heichal — five along the northern wall and five along the southern. Yet only the original Menorah of Moshe, fashioned as a single piece of beaten gold (Mikshah Achat) in the wilderness, was kindled each evening. The other nine were placed in honor, testifying to the abundance of Solomon's devotion.

Each Menorah bore seven branches and stood approximately 3.5 cubits tall. The Talmud (Menachot 98b) discusses their precise arrangement and orientation within the Sanctuary. The light of the Menorah was not needed to illuminate the chamber for G-d; rather, it served as a testimony to all humanity that the Divine Presence rested among Israel.
measurements: Height per Menorah: approx. 3.5 cubits ≈ 1.8 m
Material: from one piece of gold (Mikshah Achat)

s15.title: The Showbread Tables
s15.subtitle: Shulchanot Lechem haPanim
s15.source: Divrej haJamim Bet 4:8 · Talmud Menachot 98b · Vayikra 24:5–8
body: Ten golden tables were placed within the Heichal, though only the original table of Moshe bore the twelve loaves of the Lechem haPanim — the Showbread, or "Bread of the Presence." These twelve loaves, representing the twelve tribes of Israel, were arranged in two rows of six and replaced every Shabbat by the incoming priestly division.

The miracle of the Showbread was renowned: when the old loaves were removed after seven full days, they were found to be as warm and fresh as the moment they had been placed upon the table. This miracle served as a sign that the Divine Presence had never departed from Israel, and that G-d continued to sustain His people with invisible grace.
s15.quote: "When the loaves were removed after seven days, they were as fresh as when they were placed."

s16.title: The Golden Incense Altar
s16.subtitle: Mizbach haKetoret
body: Before the entrance to the Holy of Holies stood the Mizbach haKetoret — the Golden Incense Altar. Small in dimension but immense in significance, it was fashioned from cedarwood and overlaid with pure gold. Upon this altar, the Ketoret — the sacred incense — was burned twice daily, each morning and each evening, as commanded in Shemot 30:7–8.

The Ketoret was composed of eleven ingredients, whose precise recipe was known only to the family of Avtinas, who guarded it across generations. The Talmud (Keritot 6a) records that the fragrance of the Ketoret was so powerful that brides in Jerusalem needed no perfume — the scent carried across the entire city. The rising incense smoke symbolized the prayers of Israel ascending heavenward.

s17.title: The Golden Door to the Holy of Holies
s17.subtitle: Schaar Kodesh haKodashim
body: The entrance to the Holy of Holies was closed by doors of olive wood, carved with cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, all overlaid with gold. These doors represented the final boundary — the threshold beyond which only the Kohen Gadol was permitted to pass, and only once a year on Yom Kippur.

On that day, the High Priest would enter alone, bearing the incense and the blood of atonement, to stand before the Ark of the Covenant in the presence of the Shechina. The rest of the year, these doors remained sealed — a silent reminder that the innermost dwelling of the Divine was set apart from the world by an absolute boundary of holiness.

s18.title: The Holy Veil
s18.subtitle: Parochet
s18.source: Divrej haJamim Bet 3:14 · Shemot 26:31–33 · Talmud Chagiga 13b
body: In addition to the golden doors, a great curtain — the Parochet — separated the Heichal from the Kodesh haKodashim. Woven from threads of blue, purple, and crimson yarn, intertwined with fine linen, it bore images of cherubim worked into its fabric. The Parochet was a masterpiece of sacred textile art, a veil between the visible world and the invisible dwelling of the Divine.

According to the Talmud (Chagiga 13b), the Parochet was woven from 72 threads, each thread itself twisted from 24 individual strands, making it virtually impenetrable. Its dimensions matched the entrance it sealed: 20 cubits high and 10 cubits wide. Behind this veil lay the darkness of the Holy of Holies — a darkness that was not the absence of light, but the overwhelming fullness of Divine Presence.

s19.title: The Holy of Holies
s19.subtitle: Kodesh haKodashim
s19.source: Melachim Alef 6:16–20 · Divrej haJamim Bet 3:8–10 · Talmud Yoma 21b
body: The Kodesh haKodashim was a perfect cube — 20 cubits in every dimension — its walls, floor, and ceiling covered entirely in pure gold. Six hundred talents of gold were used for this chamber alone. Even the nails that secured the gold plates weighed 50 shekels each. No window pierced its walls; no earthly light entered. This was the most sacred space on earth — the dwelling place of the Shechina, the Divine Presence.

Within this chamber stood the Aron HaBrit — the Ark of the Covenant — containing the two stone Tablets of the Law given to Moshe at Sinai. The Talmud (Yoma 21b) records one of the great miracles of the Temple: although the Ark had physical dimensions, it occupied no measurable space. The distance from the Ark to each wall was identical to the total width of the room — as if the Ark existed beyond the ordinary laws of space.
s19.quote: "The Ark took up no space. Although it stood in the Holy of Holies, it did not diminish its dimensions."

s20.title: The Two Great Golden Angels
s20.subtitle: Cherubim
s20.source: Melachim Alef 6:23–28 · Divrej haJamim Bet 3:10–13 · Talmud Bava Batra 99a
body: Within the Holy of Holies stood two monumental Cherubim, carved from olive wood and overlaid with gold. Each Cherub stood ten cubits tall, and each wing extended five cubits, so that the combined wingspan of the two figures reached twenty cubits — spanning the entire width of the chamber, from wall to wall. Their inner wings touched at the center, above the Ark of the Covenant.

The Talmud (Bava Batra 99a) reveals a profound teaching about these Cherubim: when Israel fulfilled the will of G-d, the Cherubim turned their faces toward each other, as if embracing. When Israel strayed from the path, they turned their faces away from one another, toward the walls. The Cherubim thus served as a living barometer of the relationship between G-d and His people — a visible expression of an invisible bond.
measurements: Wing length per side: 5 cubits ≈ 2.6 m
Total wingspan: 20 cubits ≈ 10.5 m – wall to wall
s20.quote: "When Israel did the will of G-d, they turned toward each other; when not, they turned away."

s21.title: The Destruction — Churban Beit haMikdash
s21.subtitle: Tisha b'Av · The 9th of Av — eternal day of remembrance
s21.source: Melachim Bet 25:8–9 · Yechezkel 11:23 · Talmud Yoma 53b · Rambam Hilchot Melachim 11:1
body: In the year 586 BCE — the year 3,338 of the Hebrew calendar — the Babylonian armies of Nebuchadnezzar breached the walls of Jerusalem and set fire to the Beit HaMikdash on the 9th of Av. The prophet Yechezkel describes how the Shechina departed from the Temple in stages, lingering on the Mount of Olives as if reluctant to leave, before ascending heavenward. Tisha b'Av became the eternal day of national mourning.

According to tradition, King Yoshiyahu had foreseen the destruction and concealed the Ark of the Covenant, the original Menorah, and the miraculous jar of manna deep within hidden passages beneath the Temple Mount, where they await the day of ultimate redemption. The Rambam (Hilchot Melachim 11:1) teaches that the Mashiach will rebuild the Temple and gather the dispersed of Israel. The Midrash adds that the Third Temple has already been completed in heaven, fashioned from divine fire and light, and will one day descend to earth.
s21.quote: "Arise, shine, for your light has come, and the glory of the Eternal shines upon you."

blog.closing: The Temple was destroyed, but its memory was never extinguished. Every year, Jews around the world turn toward Jerusalem and whisper the same ancient hope:
